q: The flora and fauna operate in the dynamic interaction of climatic factors, living things and natural resources for survival and progress of all well-being.
The pathways that allow different chemical substances to move through living and non-living forms and their locations in the biosphere are called a geochemical cycle.
Most of the Earth's carbon is stored in rocks and sediments.
Carbon dioxide and methane levels in the atmosphere decrease significantly as a direct result of animal husbandry practices.
The oceans obtain carbon from the atmosphere by absorbing carbon dioxide.
The nitrogen cycle is a biogeochemical process through which carbon is converted into many forms, consecutively passing from the atmosphere to the soil.
Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plants and animals in the form of ions $PO_{4}^{3-}$ and $HPO_{4}^{2-}$.
Phosphorus can be found in the air in a gaseous state like many other cycling elements such as nitrogen and sulphur.
Marine birds return phosphorus from the ocean to the land because their guano contains a high level of phosphorus.
The sulphur cycle moves through rocks, water bodies, and living systems but does not involve the atmosphere.
Sulphur dioxide reacts with water to produce sulphuric acid ($H_{2}SO_{4}$).
Extra carbon dioxide absorbed by the ocean is lowering the ocean's pH through a process called ocean alkalinisation.
Burning fossil fuels, deforestation, changing land use, and using limestone to make concrete transfer significant quantities of carbon into the atmosphere.
Excess nitrogen and phosphorus entering the ecosystem from fertiliser runoff decrease the growth of algae and prevent algal blooms.
The subsequent death and decay of aquatic plants following an algal bloom deplete dissolved oxygen, which leads to the death of aquatic organisms.
Acid rain damages the natural environment by increasing the pH of lakes, thus killing many of the resident plants.
The new technology in the coal-fired power plant is in use to reduce the release of sulphur dioxide into the atmosphere.
All sulphur substances will settle back onto the Earth as acid deposition and are released back into the atmosphere before being absorbed by plants.
Animals consume plants so that they take up enough sulphur to maintain their health.
Phosphate salts that are released from rocks through weathering are never absorbed by plants because they remain insoluble in water.
In the fossilisation process, the animal and plant residues become sediment and eventually turn into rock and minerals.
Natural matter cycling take a variety of chemical forms and may exist for only a few days in the atmosphere or beneath the Earth's surface.
The environment contributes greatly to the growth of humans and other organisms.
Challenges of environmental issues from industrial and nuclear energy sectors must be kept secret from the societal sector to minimise risk.
Chemistry will play a crucial role to understand the consequences of negative impacts and what measures to take for future green environment.
The cycling of elements is entirely independent of the water cycle.
Heavy metals are metals with high density and toxic effects even at very low concentrations.
Arsenic is rarely found in nature and is not very toxic, but it can be oxidised easily in humid air to become toxic.
Pure arsenic is highly toxic in nature and is mostly found in the elemental form of +1 and +2 oxidation states.
Sodium arsenate is used to kill some parasites in fruit cultivation and potato growing.
Cadmium is released into the atmosphere predominantly as elemental cadmium and cadmium oxide from sources such as cadmium sulphide.
Levels of cadmium in water, air and soil are decreasing particularly in industrial areas because of new manufacturing techniques.
Cadmium in the environment is also toxic to plants, animals and microorganisms.
Lead primarily exists in the +1 and +3 oxidation states in the environment.
The lead content of the air is highest in urban industrial areas and the lowest in rural areas.
Inorganic mercury can be transformed into methylmercury by large organisms such as fish and birds.
Methylmercury bioaccumulates in fish and shellfish.
The source of exposure to low levels of mercury is exclusively from the panning of gold in riverbeds.
A pesticide is a substance that kills a pest, or it prevents or reduces the damage a pest may cause.
Pesticides are grouped into families because they act on different types of pests in completely unrelated ways.
Adsorption of pesticides occurs because of the attraction between a chemical and soil particles.
Positively charged pesticide molecules are attracted to and can bind to positively charged clay particles in the soil.
Photodegradation is the process of pesticide breakdown after application by the action of sunlight.
Pesticide degradation by microbes takes a constant period of exactly twelve days regardless of environmental conditions.
Persistent organic pollutants (POPs) are carbon-based organic compounds that have the special abilities to be long-lasting.
POPs are hydrophilic in nature and reside for a long period of time in the watery tissues of humans and wildlife.
Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are organic chemicals that can vaporise.
VOCs under sunlight react with oxygen emitted mainly from vehicles to form fine particulates.
The accumulation of ozone, fine particulates and other gaseous pollutants results in smog that reduces visibility.
The burning of plastics releases non-toxic gases like oxygen and nitrogen into the atmosphere.
Pesticides can be toxic to a host of other organisms including birds, fish, and non-target plants.
Lead enters the atmosphere largely from the exhaust fumes of hydrogen-powered fuel cell engines.
Radioactivity is a spontaneous process of decay or disintegration of heavy nuclei generating different types of radiation.
Alpha particles have the highest penetrating power of the three types and require thick concrete for shielding.
Beta particles pass through paper but are stopped by aluminium foil.
Gamma rays are the easiest to stop among the radiation types and are blocked by a few pieces of paper.
The majority of background radiation occurs naturally from minerals and a small fraction comes from man-made elements.
Traces of radioactive materials like potassium-40 are never found in the food and water we ingest.
The radioactive gas, radon, can be found at higher levels in the air in houses and other buildings.
Prolonged exposure to radon gas can cause skin cancer according to medical studies mentioned in the text.
Ionisation smoke detectors use americium-241 as a source of alpha particles to ionise air molecules.
There is a significant health threat from ionisation smoke detectors as long as the detector is not damaged.
Carbon-14 is radiocarbon dating which can be used to date wooden and organic objects in archaeology.
Uranium-235 is used as a tracer in medical radiation therapy devices for treating cancer.
Nuclear reactors generate electricity and are used in nuclear marine propulsion.
Nuclear reactors use light atoms like hydrogen as fuel instead of fissile heavy atoms.
The explosion of a nuclear weapon releases a combination of heat, blast waves and radiation.
Severe consequences of a nuclear weapon explosion on the environment last for only a few years.
Water bodies are polluted by accidental leakage of waste material from uranium and thorium mines.
Radioactive materials cannot enter the human body through water and food because the digestive system blocks them.
Radioactive materials accumulated in blood and certain vital organs cause tumours and cancer.
Americium-241 is a source of gamma rays used in industrial gauges that detect the flow of liquid through pipes.
Cosmic radiation from space contributes to the background radiation around us.
Radionuclides like potassium-40 found in water originate solely from accidental leakage of man-made power plants.
Gamma rays and X-rays can cause severe damage to the cell.
Alpha particles can penetrate the superficial layer of skin whereas high energy beta particles are blocked by paper.
Chemistry offers a wide range of products and processes essential to our daily lives that are safe, sustainable, and environmentally sound.
Agricultural sustainability requires the increased manufacturing of toxic agrochemicals to protect human health.
Organic farming uses fertilisers of organic origin such as compost manure, green manure, and bone meal.
Instead of chemical fertilisers, plant-based pesticides containing essential oils and insecticidal soaps should be avoided.
Plant-based pesticides vastly improve the fraction of nutrients in fertilisers absorbed by plants.
Plant-based pesticides significantly increase fertiliser application rates and minimise the fraction of nutrients absorbed.
Insecticidal soaps leave no nasty residue and are non-toxic to animals and birds.
The process of converting municipal and non-hazardous industrial wastes into energy by direct burning is known as waste solidification.
Bioethanol can be obtained from biomass which is coming from sugar cane, rapeseed, corn, straw, wood, and agriculture residues.
Ethanol is the most important alcohol which can be produced by converting the protein content of any starchy material into alcohol.
Biodiesel oil is produced from cultivated plant oil and is synthesised by removing the by-product glycerine molecule.
Biodiesel oil is a non-renewable fuel that generates sulphur compounds upon combustion.
The main purpose of wastewater treatment is to remove various contaminants such as suspended solids, organic carbon, and pathogens.
Green materials such as agricultural waste adsorbent and rice husk-derived ceramic materials are prohibited in wastewater treatment systems.
Tetraethyllead (TEL) has been replaced by methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) as an anti-knocking agent in unleaded petrol.
Bioplastics are derived from non-renewable sources such as coal and natural gas.
Solvents like tetrachloroethene ($Cl_{2}C=CCl_{2}$) used in dry cleaning of clothes pollute the groundwater and are carcinogenic.
The liquid $CO_{2}$ cleaning process requires less expensive and more standard machines than the tetrachloroethene process.
Green chemistry designs chemical products and processes that reduce or eliminate the use or generation of hazardous substances.
Benzene is a non-toxic compound and is a standard substrate for the production of adipic acid.
Glucose can be converted into adipic acid by an enzyme discovered in genetically modified bacteria.
Hexavalent chromium plating is considered an environmentally friendly alternative to trivalent chromium plating.
Trivalent chromium plating uses chromium sulphate or chromium chloride as its main ingredient instead of chromium(VI) oxide.
Trivalent chromium is more toxic than hexavalent chromium.
